articles.title: Is It Really Rude to Kick Out a Houseguest Who’s Overstayed Their Welcome?
articles.content:
我十六岁那年，最好的朋友交了个男朋友。一天下午，我和她还有她男朋友一起待在她家，我们正聊着那些现在想来肯定很重要但实际上毫无意义的废话，这时他们开始暗示我也许该回家了。我完全没有接收到这些暗示。于是他们做了青少年会做的事——就在我旁边的沙发上开始热烈地亲吻起来。然而，我依然没有接收到这些暗示。直到他们躺到沙发上、拉过一条毯子盖住自己，我才恍然大悟，顿感恐惧：我成了世界上最迟钝的电灯泡。

我真希望这只是个别事件。但对于一个在晚上十点左右迎来第二精力高峰、热爱深夜长谈的夜猫子来说：偶尔成为“那种客人”的情况确实存在。最近一次，我和一位朋友聊到深夜——她看起来听得很投入，不断点头，话不多——直到我听到一声轻柔的鼾声。我还以为她是个超级好的倾听者，结果发现她不知道什么时候已经睡着了。我悄悄离开，至今都没有完全从那份羞耻中恢复过来。

以上种种只想说明：在这场礼仪博弈中，我有切身体会。我以深度和细节理解这个困境的两面，这种理解只能来自（令人尴尬地）来之不易的智慧。

所以让我们深入探讨一下。告诉客人该走了，真的很失礼吗？

我会直说：你的家不是餐厅，门上没有打烊时间。但这不意味着不应该有打烊时间。主人也是人——要上班的人、有孩子的人、要遛狗的人、要按时服药的人，还有社交能量有限、在开始暗暗怨恨客厅里所有人之前需要休息的人。

而且数据支持这一点，尤其是对于留宿的客人。根据2025年Talker Research和Avocado Green Mattress对2000名美国人进行的调查，近三分之一的主人一旦觉得客人待得太久就会开始暗示。而22%的主人则会选择直接说出来。对于留宿客人，平均六天后主人就准备好送客了。（不过姻亲只给五天。你自己下结论吧。）

即使是最殷勤的主人也有极限——了解自己的底线并没错。礼仪专家简·戈斯曾告诉我：“在大家还开心的时候离开，比待到超过时限、以不愉快的结局收场要好。”阿门，简。

反驳的声音更温和，但同样真实。真正的连接是稀有的。一场愉快的对话、一场有趣的游戏之夜、一顿美好的晚餐，这些都不是能人为制造的。所以当你正沉浸在其中的时候，为了夺回沙发而打断它，可能会让你感觉像是在给本应无价的时刻标上价格。

当然，不是所有待到很晚的理由都同等合理。周二晚上的晚餐拖长了和周末与最亲密的人聚会（你们一年才见一次）是有区别的。大学室友和丈夫那位死活听不懂暗示的工作同事也是不一样的。而且还有一个真正的问题：客人是否知道气氛已经变了——因为，正如我亲身证明的那样，有些人读空气的能力绝对堪忧。（我愿意相信作为真正的成年人我已经进步了，但当我问丈夫时，他清了清嗓子，去查看他的植物了，所以……）

知道如何做一个好客人，包括知道何时离开——而责任并不完全在客人身上。回避确定结束日期的主人，是在为怨恨的缓慢积累埋下隐患，这可能会毁掉关系。

这是每个人都会跳过、直接用夸张的哈欠和希望客人自己明白的部分。让我们来谈谈策略。

真正失礼的事情是让你的紧张情绪不断累积，直到你开始在客人面前大力打扫卫生、带着明显压抑的怒火把碗碟放进洗碗机，而他们还傻乎乎地坐在沙发上。我知道这一点是因为我见证过这种做法在凌晨两点造成的后果。我们的一位客人误以为这种家务活是明确的信号，感到极大的冒犯，然后在半夜不辞而别。说真的，我们醒来时，人已经不见了。

即使是最周全的客人礼仪指南，也把责任放在双方：客人要会读空气，主人要会用嘴说话，而不是用家电。

不，告诉客人该走了并不是失礼。当带着温暖来做这件事时，它实际上是一份礼物——对他们来说，因为现在他们知道了；对你来说，因为你终于可以上床睡觉了。（还因为你不用目睹他们几乎在你腿上亲热。我当时简直要死了。）

真正的礼仪问题不是你能不能这样做。而是你能不能在不让他们感到不受欢迎的情况下做到。答案是可以的。要直接。要温暖。要具体。（“我太累了，明天还有重要的事”比对着时钟沉重地叹气效果好得多。）而且要在你已经对着群聊草拟了一条阴阳怪气的消息吐槽之前就这样做。

如果他们在那之后选择不高兴地离开，或者气冲冲地离开——那是他们的问题。

你有无法停止反复琢磨的社交困境吗？发邮件至advice@tmbi.com，或者在Instagram上给夏洛特发消息：@CharlotteHiltonAndersen。
articles.summary:
When I was 16, my best friend got a boyfriend. One afternoon, I was at her house hanging out with both of them, deeply engrossed in what I’m sure was a very important conversation about nothing, when they started dropping hints that maybe I should head home. I did not pick up on these hints. So they did what teens do and started aggressively making out on the couch right next to me. And yet, I still did not pick up on these hints. It wasn’t until they laid down on the couch and pulled a blanket over them that I finally understood, with dawning horror, that I had become the world’s most oblivious third wheel.

I wish I could tell you that was an isolated incident. But here’s the thing about being a night owl who gets her second wind around 10 p.m. and loves a deep, rambling conversation: I have occasionally been That Guest. More recently, I was chatting late into the night with a friend—she seemed so engaged, nodding along, not saying much—until I heard a soft sound that was unmistakably snoring. I thought she was just a really great listener, but it turns out she had been asleep for who knows how long. I let myself out quietly and have never fully recovered from the shame.

All of which is to say: I have skin in this particular etiquette game. I understand both sides of this dilemma with a depth and specificity that can only come from hard-won (embarrassingly won?) wisdom.

So let’s get into it. Is it really rude to tell your guests it’s time to go?

Get Reader’s Digest’s Read Up newsletter for more etiquette, humor, cleaning, travel, tech and fun facts all week long.

The case for kicking them out

I’m going to be blunt: Your home is not a restaurant, and there are no closing hours posted on the door. But that doesn’t mean there shouldn’t be a closing time. Hosts are people too—people with jobs, kids, dogs to walk, medications to take at a reasonable hour and a finite amount of social energy before they start quietly resenting everyone in their living room.

And the data backs this up, especially when it comes to houseguests who are staying overnight. According to a 2025 survey of 2,000 Americans conducted by Talker Research and Avocado Green Mattress, nearly a third of hosts will start dropping hints once they feel a guest has overstayed. And 22% will just come out and say it directly. For overnight guests, the magic number is six days before the average host is ready to see the back of someone’s suitcase. (Though in-laws get only five days. Draw your own conclusions.)

Even the most gracious host has a limit—and there’s nothing rude about knowing where yours is. As etiquette expert Jan Goss once told me: “Better to leave when everyone’s happy than to overstay your welcome and end it on a bad note.” Amen, Jan.

The case for letting it ride

The counterargument is softer, but real. Connection is rare. A good conversation, a hilarious game night and a fantastic dinner are not connections you can manufacture. So when you’re in the middle of one, cutting it off to reclaim your couch can feel like putting a price tag on a moment that should be priceless.

Of course, not all reasons to stay late are created equally. There’s a difference between a Tuesday-night dinner that ran long and a weekend gathering with your closest people who you see only once a year. There’s a difference between your college roommate and your husband’s work colleague who just will not take the hint. And there’s a real question of whether the guest even knows the vibe has shifted—because, as I have personally demonstrated, some of us are absolutely terrible at reading the room. (I’d like to think I’ve gotten better now that I’m a real adult, but when I asked my husband, he cleared his throat and went to check on his plants, so …)

The gray areas

  • The deep-conversation trap: This is the one that gets me every time. You’re in the middle of a genuinely meaningful exchange—someone’s talking about something real and vulnerable—and suddenly you’re supposed to announce that it’s time to wrap up because you have a spin class in the morning? It feels cruel. There’s no clean answer here; sometimes connection wins and you sleep in. But a deep conversation is also not a hostage situation, and a good host is allowed to call it.
  • Weeknight vs. weekend: On a Saturday night, 1 a.m. is fair game for a lively group of friends. On a Wednesday, it is absolutely acceptable—arguably mandatory—to wrap things up by 10 p.m. Things end even earlier if young children are involved. After all, school nights affect parents as much as kids!
  • How close you are: Your college roommate gets more runway than your husband’s co-worker. Your closest friend who’s going through something hard gets more runway than the neighbor who just likes your snacks. Relationship proximity is a real variable. But paradoxically, the closer you are, the easier it also is to just say, “OK, I’m done. I love you. Go home.”
  • When the guest is staying with you: This is a whole different category of awkward, and it has a particular subspecies that deserves its own mention: the guest who tells you when they’re arriving but not when they’re leaving. “We’ll see how it goes and how we’re feeling!” is a sentence that sounds breezy and spontaneous but is actually a small act of terrorism. I have a specific set of family members who are notorious for doing this. I will not say which ones. I will say that the open-ended visit is the direct cause of approximately 60% of all passive-aggressive dishwasher-loading in America. (You know you’ve really messed up if the host starts vacuuming.)

Knowing how to be a good houseguest includes knowing when to leave—and the responsibility isn’t entirely on the guest. Hosts who avoid pinning down an end date are setting themselves up for a slow build of resentment that can ruin relationships.

How to ask them to leave

Here’s the part everyone skips over in favor of yawning theatrically and hoping for the best. Let’s talk tactics.

  • Start by setting an end time. This is the prequel to asking them to leave, but do it right, and you may not even need these other suggestions. Good party etiquette includes setting expectations before guests arrive, which is the most underrated move a host can make. End times exist. Use them. Actually put them on the invite or in the text.
  • Skip the hints. Yawning, starting to clean up, making pointed comments about your early morning—these work on some guests and sail right over others. Unlike the Irish exit, which requires only your own nerve, the hint approach depends entirely on the other person’s self-awareness. That is a variable quantity, as I have personally and repeatedly demonstrated.
  • Be warm and direct. “I’ve had such a great time, but I’m officially running out of steam. Can we do this again soon?” is not a rejection. It’s a sentence. It works. You can even make a joke of the delivery—”OK, I love you, but I’m kicking you out. Big day tomorrow”—which signals warmth while still being unambiguous. The goal is to make them feel good about the evening even as you’re showing them the door.
  • For overnight guests, set the end date up front. And this needs to happen before they arrive, not the morning they’re supposed to leave. It’s not rude to say: “We’re so excited to have you—we were thinking through the weekend. Does Sunday checkout work?” It’s a gift to everyone involved. And it closes the door on “We’ll see how we’re feeling,” which, I cannot stress enough, is a one-way ticket to crazytown.
  • Find the right opening. A natural pause in conversation, the end of a meal, someone getting up to use the bathroom—these are your windows. Don’t try to interrupt the climax of a story. Wait for a beat, then move toward the door yourself. Body language does half the work.

When you actually cross the line into rudeness

The one thing that is genuinely rude is letting your tension build until you’re doing an aggressive cleanup around your guest, loading the dishwasher with thinly veiled anger while they sit obliviously on the couch. I know this because I have witnessed the 2 a.m. consequences of exactly this approach. We had a guest who was staying with us mistake that household chore for a pointed signal, took massive offense and then left in the middle of the night without a word. Seriously, we woke up, and they were just gone.

Even the most thoughtful houseguest etiquette guidance puts the burden on both parties: guests to read the room and hosts to use their words rather than their appliances.

The verdict

No, it is not rude to tell your guests it’s time to go. When done with warmth, it’s actually a gift—to them, because now they know, and to you, because you can finally go to bed. (And because you don’t have to witness them making out practically on your lap. I just about died.)

The real etiquette question isn’t whether you can do it. It’s whether you can do it without making them feel unwanted. And the answer is yes. Be direct. Be warm. Be specific. (“I’m exhausted and I have a big day tomorrow” lands better than a vague, heavy sigh in the direction of the clock). And do it before you’ve mentally drafted a passive-aggressive text to your group chat about it.

If they choose to take it poorly after that and leave your house in a huff—or in the middle of the night—that’s on them.

Have a social situation you can’t stop ruminating on? Email us at advice@tmbi.com, or message Charlotte on Instagram at @CharlotteHiltonAndersen.

Why trust us

Reader’s Digest has published hundreds of etiquette stories that help readers navigate communication in a changing world. We regularly cover topics such as the best messages to send for any occasion, polite habits that aren’t as polite as they seem, email and texting etiquette, business etiquette, tipping etiquette, travel etiquette and more. We’re committed to producing high-quality content by writers with expertise and experience in their field in consultation with relevant, qualified experts. We rely on reputable primary sources, including government and professional organizations and academic institutions as well as our writers’ personal experiences where appropriate. We verify all facts and data, back them with credible sourcing and revisit them over time to ensure they remain accurate and up to date. Read more about our team, our contributors and our editorial policies.

Source:

  • Talker Research: “How long is too long? Hosts say six days is the limit for houseguests”

Is It Rude to Share Someone Else's News?

Is It Rude to Buy Food for a Potluck?

Is It Rude Not to Chip in for the Group Gift?

The post Is It Really Rude to Kick Out a Houseguest Who’s Overstayed Their Welcome? appeared first on Reader's Digest.

articles.title: A Viral TikTok Hack Promises Better Sleep During Flights. Here’s Why You Absolutely Shouldn’t Try It—And What to Do Instead
articles.content:
我的伴侣经常出差，几乎随时随地都能睡着。但我呢？就没那么幸运了。虽然我喜欢旅行，每年都会乘坐多次国内和国际航班，但我特别不擅长在飞机上睡觉。有一次深夜飞往苏格兰和意大利的航班，我真的需要睡觉，结果两趟加起来只睡了大约30分钟。我知道肯定不止我一个人这样。

我们都希望能找到在飞机上安稳睡觉的秘诀——那些狭小的座位跟我们舒适的床铺相比简直是天壤之别！最近在TikTok上走红的旅行小技巧声称正好能做到这一点。但问题是：专家表示这不安全（另外，这也相当不礼貌）。我们采访了一位前空乘人员、一位睡眠专家和一位血管外科医生，来解释为什么他们认为这个技巧不值得尝试。

**@zoreandtomek** 把安全带绑在腿上 ✈️♬ 原声 – zoreandtomek

这个TikTok小技巧相当简单（当然，前提是你的腿能做到这个姿势）：不要把脚放在地板上，而是把膝盖收到胸前，脚底踩在座位上。然后，为了固定弯曲的双腿，把安全带扣在脚踝上而不是跨过臀部。TikTok创作者声称这个姿势更舒服，能让他们睡得更香。

但专家表示，在飞行过程中保持这种姿势可能有危险（还有其他问题），他们不鼓励旅行者尝试。让我们来看看其中的原因。

专家指出了为什么这样坐着不是个好主意的几个原因。以下是你需要了解的关于这个可疑病毒式传播小技巧的事情。

“人们低估了意外颠簸有多剧烈，”在加拿大航空担任了21年空乘人员、 mostly飞长途国际航班的泰森·萨德勒（Tyson Sadler）说道。“如果你的腿被绑在胸前，你就不是处于稳定的坐姿，可能会很快被甩向一侧。”

你还让空乘人员更难工作。“在紧急情况下，空乘人员需要乘客坐直、系好安全带、能够立即行动，”萨德勒补充道。“把自己用安全带绑成这样会在分秒必争的情况下造成延误和混乱。”

保持这种姿势对血液循环也不好，可能会增加危险血凝块的风险——尤其是在长途航班上，你本来就连续几个小时几乎无法动弹。“长时间空中旅行已经会增加血凝块的风险，因为长时间坐着、活动受限、脱水以及机舱压力变化，”东卡罗来纳大学血管外科主任蒙尼尔·豪拉尼（Mounir Haurani）医学博士说道。

即使我们在飞行的大部分时间都坐着，我们仍然会在座位上轻微地动一动、起身去洗手间，甚至伸展和调整腿部姿势。豪拉尼博士说，我们睡觉时也会稍微动一动。“遵循这个'旅行小技巧'可能会让人在更长时间内更不愿意动，可能会进一步增加血液循环问题的风险，比如深静脉血栓形成，即腿部形成血凝块，”他解释道。

以膝盖抵胸的紧密蜷缩姿势坐着也会给关节、肌肉和血管带来压力，豪拉尼博士指出。“血液如何从腿部流回心脏的生理和物理机制很复杂，依赖于呼吸带来的压力变化、腿部肌肉的泵送动作、心脏的泵送以及一系列瓣膜，”他说。“以这些角度弯曲腿部和臀部几乎会破坏所有这些机制。”

像这样把自己绑起来对其他乘客来说也是不体贴的。“一旦有人把腿抬到座位上，他们通常开始侵占旁边的空间，”萨德勒说。“在满员的航班上，这对周围的每个人来说都很烦人。”

而且真的，没人想碰到你的脚。“把鞋放在座位上也是乘客一直抱怨的事情，”萨德勒说。“飞机座位是共享的公共空间，人们通常不希望鞋子放在他们坐着或放置手臂的地方。”

幸运的是，有一些安全、有效的方法可以在飞机上睡觉，不会打扰其他乘客，神经学家、睡眠专家、著有两本睡眠方面书籍的W·克里斯托弗·温特（W. Christopher Winter）医学博士说道。“我经常出差，我认为制定一个有条理的计划至关重要，”温特博士说。他随身携带三件必备品来帮助他在航班上睡觉：耳塞、眼罩和颈枕。（他喜欢NapAnywhere颈枕，可以折平且非常轻便。）

如果他坐的不是能倾斜的座位，温特博士会选择靠窗的位置。他通常左侧睡，所以他尽量选择飞机左侧的座位，这样他可以模仿自然的睡眠姿势。“飞行前几天，我会查看是否有靠窗座位旁边是空的，”他说。“很多时候，如果你这样做，你会为自己多争取一些空间。”

萨德勒赞同温特博士的想法。“处理长途航班最好的乘客通常很简单：颈枕、眼罩、补充水分和舒适的衣物，”他说。“靠窗座位通常最适合睡觉，因为你不会一直被爬过去，而且可以靠在飞机舱壁上。”

所有专家都建议在飞行前后保持充足的水分——但这并不意味着喝很多含咖啡因的饮料或酒精。“两者都会导致脱水并扰乱睡眠质量，”豪拉尼博士说。他提供了一个最后的建议：轻装上阵。“只带绝对必要的物品上飞机，”他说。“你座位下方的空间对于让你的小腿和大腿肌肉活动非常宝贵。在长途旅行中，多花点钱托运行李、保持地板畅通，可能是避免血凝块和安全旅程的区别所在。”

**关于专家**

《读者文摘》已经发布了数百篇旅行故事，帮助读者安全、轻松、经济地探索世界。我们经常报道诸如最佳旅游地点（以及最佳旅游时间）、快速通过安检的技巧和窍门、空乘人员的秘密、酒店房间的小妙招等内容。我们致力于制作由具有专业知识和经验的作家撰写的高质量内容，并咨询相关合格专家。我们依靠政府、专业组织和学术机构等可靠的主要来源，以及作家在适当情况下的个人经历。我们验证所有事实和数据，用可靠来源支持，并随着时间推移进行回顾，以确保它们保持准确和最新。了解更多关于我们团队、我们的撰稿人和我们的编辑政策的信息。
articles.summary:
My partner travels a ton and can sleep pretty much anytime, anywhere. But me? Not so much. Even though I love traveling and take several cross-country and international flights a year, I am terrible at sleeping on planes. On overnight flights to Scotland and Italy, when I really had to sleep, I managed to doze for a grand total of about 30 minutes both times. And I know I’m not alone.

We’d all love to unlock the secret to sleeping soundly on planes—those small seats are a far cry from our comfy beds!—and a recent travel hack that’s gone viral on TikTok promises to do exactly that. There’s just one problem: Experts say it’s not safe (plus, it’s also kind of rude). We spoke to a former flight attendant, a sleep expert and a vascular physician to explain why they don’t think this is a tip you should try.

Get Reader’s Digest’s Read Up newsletter for more travel, cleaning, humor, tech and fun facts all week long.

What’s the viral hack?

@zoreandtomekWrap the seatbelt around your legs ✈️♬ original sound – zoreandtomek

The TikTok hack is fairly simple (for those who can get their legs in this position, of course): Instead of keeping your feet on the floor, you pull your knees up to your chest and rest the bottoms of your feet on the seat. Then, to hold your bent legs in place, you buckle the seat belt (or “feet belt,” as some are calling it) around your ankles instead of across your hips. TikTok creators claim this position is more comfortable and helps them sleep more soundly.

But experts say keeping the legs in this position during a flight is potentially dangerous (among other things), and they discourage travelers from trying it. Let’s get into the reasons why.

Why is this such a bad idea?

Experts cite several reasons why sitting like this is not a good idea. Here’s what you should know about this highly suspect viral hack.

It’s dangerous

“People underestimate how violent unexpected turbulence can be,” says Tyson Sadler, who worked as a flight attendant for Air Canada for 21 years and flew mostly long-haul international flights. “If your legs are strapped up against your chest, you’re not seated in a stable position, and you can get thrown sideways very quickly.”

You’re also making it harder for flight attendants to do their jobs. “In an emergency, flight attendants need passengers upright, properly belted and able to move immediately,” Sadler adds. “Wrapping yourself into the seat belt creates delays and confusion when seconds matter.”

It’s not healthy

Keeping your legs in this position is also not good for circulation and could raise the risk of a dangerous blood clot—especially during long flights when you’re already mostly immobile for hours at a time. “Prolonged air travel already increases the risk of blood clots due to extended sitting, restricted movement, dehydration and cabin-pressure changes,” says Mounir Haurani, MD, chief of the division of vascular surgery at East Carolina University.

Even when we’re seated for the majority of a flight, we still fidget and shift in our seats a little bit, get up to use the restroom and even stretch and reposition our legs. We also move a little while we’re sleeping, Dr. Haurani says. “Following this ‘travel hack’ may make individuals even less likely to move for an extended period of time, potentially further increasing the risk of circulation issues, such as deep vein thrombosis, in which a blood clot forms in the leg,” he explains.

Sitting in a tightly compressed position with the knees at the chest can also strain the joints, muscles and blood vessels, according to Dr. Haurani. “The physiology and physics of how blood gets out of your legs to your heart is complex but relies on pressure changes from breathing, the pumping motion of your leg muscles, your heart pumping and a series of valves,” he says. “Folding your legs and hips at these angles disrupts almost all of these mechanisms.”

It’s just bad manners

Strapping yourself in like this is inconsiderate to other passengers. “Once someone pulls their legs up onto the seat, they usually start spilling into the space beside them,” Sadler says. “On a full flight, that becomes frustrating for everyone around them.”

And really, no one wants to deal with your feet. “Putting shoes on the seat is also something passengers complain about constantly,” Sadler says. “Airplane seats are shared public spaces, and people generally don’t want shoes where they’re sitting or resting their arms.”

What are better ideas for sleeping on a plane?

Fortunately, there are safe, effective ways to sleep on planes that won’t disrupt other passengers, says W. Christopher Winter, MD, a neurologist, sleep specialist and author of two books about sleep. “I travel a ton, and I think having an organized plan is essential,” Dr. Winter says. He carries three essentials to help him sleep on flights: earplugs, an eye mask and a neck pillow. (He likes the NapAnywhere pillow, which folds flat and is super light.)

If he’s not in a seat that reclines, Dr. Winter chooses a window seat. He normally sleeps on his left side, so he tries to pick a seat on the left side of the plane whenever possible so he can mimic his natural sleep position. “A few days before the flight, I look to see if there’s a window seat that has a seat next to it empty,” he says. “Many times, if you do this, you will buy yourself some extra room.”

Sadler echoes Dr. Winter’s ideas. “Passengers who handle long-haul flights best usually keep it simple: neck pillow, eye mask, hydration and comfortable layers,” he says. “Window seats are usually best for sleeping because you’re not being climbed over constantly and can lean against the wall of the aircraft.”

And all of the experts recommend staying adequately hydrated before and during flights—and that doesn’t mean lots of caffeinated drinks or alcohol. “Both will contribute to dehydration and disrupt sleep quality,” Dr. Haurani says. He offers one final tip: Pack light. “Only bring the absolute essentials on board with you,” he says. “The space under your seat is valuable space for letting your calf and thigh muscles flex. On long trips, spending the extra money to check a bag and keep the floor clear could be the difference between blood clots and a safe journey.”

RELATED:

  • Always Bring This Item on International Flights—It Can Save You a Surprising Amount of Time!
  • Ew! This Part of the Airplane Is Crawling with Germs—And You’re Going to Touch It
  • Report: This Is the Safest Place to Travel in the World—And It’s Packed with Loads of Bucket-List Attractions

About the experts

  • Tyson Sadler was a flight attendant for Air Canada for 21 years. Based in Toronto, he primarily worked long-haul international flights to London, Frankfurt, Tel Aviv, Delhi and other destinations.
  • Mounir Haurani, MD, MPH, is chief of the division of vascular surgery at East Carolina University in Greenville, North Carolina.
  • W. Christopher Winter, MD, is a neurologist, sleep specialist and author of The Rested Child and The Sleep Solution. He’s also the host of the podcast Sleep Unplugged with Dr. Chris Winter.

Why trust us

Reader’s Digest has published hundreds of travel stories that help readers explore the world safely, easily and affordably. We regularly cover topics such as the best places to visit (and the best times to visit them), tips and tricks to zoom through airport security, flight-attendant secrets, hotel-room hacks and more. We’re committed to producing high-quality content by writers with expertise and experience in their field in consultation with relevant, qualified experts. We rely on reputable primary sources, including government and professional organizations and academic institutions as well as our writers’ personal experiences where appropriate. We verify all facts and data, back them with credible sourcing, and revisit them over time to ensure they remain accurate and up to date. Read more about our team, our contributors and our editorial policies.

Sources:

  • Tyson Sadler, former flight attendant for Air Canada; email interview, May 16, 2026
  • Mounir Haurani, MD, MPH, chief of the Division of Vascular Surgery at East Carolina University; email interview, May 18, 2026
  • W. Christopher Winter, MD, neurologist, sleep specialist and author of The Sleep Solution; email interview, May 18, 2026

  • TikTok: “Airplane seat buckle around feet”

articles.title: The Royal Family Knows a Guy Named Steve Really Well—Here’s Who He Is and What Happened to Him
articles.content:
王室成员会见了很多人，但只有少数人能走进宫殿围墙之内。然而，昔日有位名叫史帝夫的家伙做到了：他被这个星球上最著名的家族接纳，享受着君主制的一切特权。如果你现在正挠头（哪个史帝夫？），你并不孤单。

那么这位史帝夫究竟是谁？他又是如何赢得温莎家族青睐的？原来他与宫殿有着秘密联系。王室传记作家罗伯特·乔布森在一本书中写了这个话题，我们将在此揭露这些秘密。继续阅读，看看史帝夫是谁，以及他是如何获得如此接近王室的特权的。

订阅《读者文摘》Read Up新闻通讯，获取更多关于王室、清洁妙招、幽默、旅行、科技和趣味知识的资讯，整周不停。

是威廉王子！没错，史帝夫正是我们英国王位继承人的秘密化身，当时他是苏格兰圣安德鲁斯大学的学生。显然，年轻的威廉在2001年开始学习艺术史（后来改为地理）时，非常想融入其他同学，所以他选择了“史帝夫”这个假名，在新结识的大学伙伴中使用。

据乔布森记载，他实际上是用“威廉·威尔士”这个名字注册的，但后来改了名字以避免在同学中显得突出。“在朋友们中间，他决定使用'史帝夫'这个假名，这是一种奇怪的尝试，想要低调行事，”乔布森在其著作《凯特：威尔士王妃》中写道。据这位作者说，威廉曾说：“那些想利用我、想从我身上捞好处的人，我能很快察觉，然后很快就会疏远他们。我不傻。”

还要应付那难以满足的英国媒体。《每日邮报》报道，威廉采用这个假名是为了能够拥有正常的大学生活，避免媒体关注。虽然我们赞赏这种努力，但不太可能世界上最有价值的单身汉（当时）能够很快淡出人们的视线——不管有没有假名。

嗯，不完全是，但乔布森说王室和媒体之间有一项协议，在威廉大学期间配合这场戏。与英国新闻投诉委员会达成了一项协议，禁止未经许可拍摄他的任何照片，除了少数几次有组织的拍摄（如2001年9月入学第一天的拍摄），威廉基本上是独处的。

你绝对猜不到是哪个媒体打破了这个协议：爱德华王子！威廉那个调皮的叔叔，伊丽莎白女王和菲利普亲王最小的儿子，当时经营着自己的电视制作公司Ardent Productions，他派了一个摄制组去拍他的侄子。“威廉打电话向他父亲抱怨，”蒂娜·布朗在《王室文件》中写道。“查尔斯王子对此番兄弟背叛怒不可遏。”

显然是的——凯特也用过。他们约会期间，威廉和凯特会在巴尔莫勒尔、希格罗夫或桑德林厄姆的乡间别墅共度周末，如果入住酒店，他们会用一个非常普通的化名。“偶尔他们用'史密斯先生和太太'这个化名入住酒店——这无疑骗不了任何人，”乔布森写道。

据《每日邮报》报道，2007年这对情侣复合后去塞舌尔度假时，他们还用“马丁和罗斯玛丽·米德尔顿”这个名字登记酒店。

他们确实使用化名！这完全是可以理解的，是为了隐私和安全。如果王室成员想光顾餐厅、酒店或剧院，如果用自己的名字预订，就会引起巨大的骚动，也可能带来安全风险。所以王室成员和其他名人一样经常使用假名，王室助手和安保人员也会用代号称呼他们。以下是一些他们的别名。

《读者文摘》已经发布了数百篇关于英国王室的故事，提供对君主制引人入胜一面的幕后视角。我们定期报道的话题包括最新王室新闻、悠久传统的历史和意义，以及每位王室成员——从伊丽莎白女王的每日零食到威廉王子关于家庭生活的自白——的日常趣事。我们致力于由具有专业领域知识和经验的作家在相关合格专家的咨询下创作高质量内容。我们依靠包括政府和专业组织以及学术机构在内的可靠一手来源，以及作家在适当情况下的个人经历。我们核实所有事实和数据，用可靠来源支持，并随着时间推移重新审视，确保它们保持准确和最新。了解更多关于我们团队、撰稿人和编辑政策的信息。

本文《王室与一位名叫史帝夫的男士非常熟悉——他是谁？后来怎样了？》首次发表于《读者文摘》。
articles.summary:
Members of the royal family meet a lot of people, but only a few get to go behind the palace walls. Back in the day, though, there was a guy named Steve who did just that: He was embraced by the most famous family on the planet and enjoyed all the perks of the monarchy. If you’re scratching your head right now (which one was Steve?), you’re not alone.

So just who is this Steve, and what did he do to win over the Windsors? Turns out he had secret connections to the palace all along. Royal biographer Robert Jobson wrote about this subject in one of his books, and we’re spilling the secrets ahead. Read on to discover who Steve is and how he got such prime access to the royals.

Get Reader’s Digest’s Read Up newsletter for more royals, cleaning, humor, travel, tech and fun facts all week long.

So who is Steve?

Prince William! Yes, Steve was the unlikely alter ego of our very own heir to the British throne while he was a student at the University of St. Andrews in Scotland. Apparently, young Wills really wanted to fit in with other students when he began his art history (later geography) degree back in 2001, so he chose the pseudonym Steve to use around his new college buddies.

According to Jobson, he actually enrolled under the name William Wales but changed his name to avoid standing out from the other students. “Among friends, he decided to use the pseudonym ‘Steve’ in a bizarre attempt to stay under the radar,” Jobson writes in his book Catherine: The Princess of Wales. According to the author, William said: “People who try to take advantage of me and get a piece of me, I spot it quickly and soon go off them. I’m not stupid.”

There was also the insatiable British press to contend with. The Daily Mail reports that William adopted the pseudonym so he could have a normal college existence and avoid the media. While we applaud the effort, it’s unlikely that the world’s most eligible bachelor (at the time) could have faded into the background for long—fake name or not.

Did this really fool the press?

Um, no, but Jobson says there was an agreement between the monarchy and the media to go along with the charade while William was at university. A deal had been made with the U.K.’s Press Complaints Commission prohibiting taking any photos of him without permission, and apart from a few organized photo shoots (such as on his first day in September 2001), William was pretty much left alone.

And you’ll never guess which member of the media broke this deal: Prince Edward! William’s naughty uncle, the youngest son of Queen Elizabeth and Prince Philip, was head of his own TV production company, Ardent Productions, and sent a crew to film his nephew. “William called his father to complain,” writes Tina Brown in The Palace Papers. “Prince Charles was incandescent at this fraternal betrayal.”

Did “Steve” go by any other names?

Apparently, yes—and so did Kate. While dating, William and Kate would spend weekends at a cottage at Balmoral, Highgrove or Sandringham, and if they checked into a hotel, they would go by a very plain co-name. “Occasionally they checked into hotels using the names Mr. and Mrs. Smith—which doubtless fooled no one,” Jobson writes.

And according to the Daily Mail, when the couple got back together in 2007 and went on holiday in the Seychelles, they also used the names Martin and Rosemary Middleton to check into hotels.

Do the royals make a habit of using aliases?

They do! Understandably, it’s for privacy and security. If members of the royal family want to visit a restaurant, hotel or theater, there’s going to be a huge fuss and possible security risk if they book under their own name. So royals, like other celebrities, often use fake names, and royal aides and protection officers have code names for their charges. Here are some of their aliases.

  • William and Kate: After they were married and given the titles Duke and Duchess of Cambridge, palace aides used “DC” code names Danny Collins and Daphne Clark to refer to the couple.
  • King Charles: He’s had a whole raft of aliases, including Charles Renfrew, which Princess Diana reportedly called him before they were married. The name comes from one of his titles, Baron of Renfrew. While he was a prince, Charles also reportedly used the name Charlie Chester based on yet another title: Earl of Chester. And in the 1970s, the prince submitted a watercolor to the Royal Academy summer exhibition using the alias A.G. Carrick (using two of his names, Arthur and George, as well as Carrick from his title Earl of Carrick).
  • Charles and Camilla: They famously called each other Fred and Gladys—the initials of which Diana discovered on a bracelet destined as a gift for Camilla.
  • Harry and Meghan: The Duke and Duchess of Sussex have reportedly traveled under the names Davina Scott and David Stevens.
  • Princess Margaret: The late queen’s sister used the alias Maggie Jones to book a table at Kensington restaurant Nan’s Kitchen with Lord Snowdon, and the restaurant later renamed itself Maggie Jones’s in her honor.
  • Queen Elizabeth: In 2009, the queen booked tickets to see War Horse at a London theater with Prince Philip under the name Susan Hussey—one of her ladies-in-waiting. (The pair were regular theatergoers, we understand.) Susan wasn’t the late monarch’s only “S” alias: Her protection team used to refer to her as—wait for it—Sharon.

RELATED:

  • Here’s What One Royal *Really* Thought About Her Engagement Ring
  • Royal Scoop! Queen Elizabeth Kept a Secret Stash of This Sweet Treat in the Palace
  • Prince William Banned This Relative from His Wedding—And It’s Not Who You Think!

Why trust us

Reader’s Digest has published hundreds of stories on the British royal family, providing a behind-the-scenes look at the fascinating facets of the monarchy. We regularly cover topics including the latest royal news, the history and meaning behind time-honored traditions, and the everyday quirks of everyone’s favorite family members, from Queen Elizabeth’s daily snack to Prince William’s confessions about his home life. We’re committed to producing high-quality content by writers with expertise and experience in their field in consultation with relevant, qualified experts. We rely on reputable primary sources, including government and professional organizations and academic institutions as well as our writers’ personal experiences where appropriate. We verify all facts and data, back them with credible sourcing and revisit them over time to ensure they remain accurate and up to date. Read more about our team, our contributors and our editorial policies.

Sources:

  • Catherine, The Princess of Wales by Robert Jobson
  • The Palace Papers by Tina Brown
  • Daily Mail: “He’s Prince William to us, but at university the royal used a VERY ordinary fake name”
  • New York Times: “A King Who Actually Likes the Arts”
  • The Standard: “Maggie Jones’s: Restaurant named after Princess Margaret’s alias reopens after two years”

articles.title: The Sneaky Summer Travel Scam Hitting Inboxes Right Before Memorial Day
articles.content:
2026年预订旅行意味着你会看到收件箱里充斥着各种奇怪的邮件：收据、酒店提醒、密码重置，还有至少一封含糊其辞地声称“出了点问题”的邮件。

最后一封可能根本不是酒店发的。

网络安全公司迈克菲（McAfee）发布的一份新报告发现，虚假预订确认和旅行更新类诈骗是美国人在夏季假期前最容易中招的旅行诈骗类型之一。这些消息看起来出奇地真实，有时甚至会复制航空公司、酒店和预订网站的标志和布局。常见例子包括：

目标很简单——让旅行者适度恐慌，先点击再思考。

根据该报告，38%的旅行者表示曾遇到过旅行相关诈骗，其中41%的人遭受了经济损失，近半数人损失超过500美元。

这并非巧合。随着阵亡将士纪念日周末旅行预计将创下今年新高，数百万美国人正在为航班、公路旅行、游轮和长周末做准备。

迈克菲还发现，41%的旅行者在收到看似来自航空公司或酒店的消息时会直接信任，而不事先核实。最安全的做法是什么？永远不要直接从消息中点击链接，即使它看起来合法。正确的做法是单独打开航空公司、酒店或预订应用程序，在那里查看预订信息。如果真有问题，通常也会在官方账户中显示。

无论做什么，都不要冲动地在谷歌上搜索客服电话。诈骗者有时会创建虚假的客服热线，专门从急于快速解决问题的焦虑旅行者那里收集支付信息。基本上，如果一条旅行消息突然让你的心跳加速，那就是应该放慢脚步的时刻。

真正的航空公司可能会延误你的航班。诈骗者却想让你在注意到邮件地址结尾是“support-air-help247.net”这种奇怪域名之前就惊慌失措。
articles.summary:
Booking a trip in 2026 means watching your inbox fill up with a strange mix of receipts, hotel reminders, password resets and at least one email vaguely insisting something is wrong.

That last one might not actually be from the hotel.

A new report from cybersecurity company McAfee found that fake booking confirmations and travel updates are among the travel scams Americans say they’re most likely to fall for ahead of summer vacation season. The messages can look surprisingly real, sometimes copying the exact logos and layouts of airlines, hotels and booking sites. Common examples include:

  • A “your reservation needs verification” email
  • A text claiming your flight time or gate just changed
  • A hotel notice saying your payment failed
  • A warning that your booking will be canceled in minutes if you don’t “act now”

The goal is simple. Panic the traveler just enough to make them click first and think later.

According to the report, 38% of travelers say they’ve encountered a travel-related scam, and 41% of those people took a financial hit, with nearly half losing more than $500.

It’s not a coincidence either. Millions of Americans are packing for flights, road trips, cruises and long weekends as Memorial Day weekend travel is expected to hit record levels this year.

McAfee also found that 41% of travelers trust messages that appear to come from airlines or hotels without double-checking them first. The safest move? Never click directly from the message itself, even if it looks legitimate. Instead, open the airline, hotel or booking app separately and check the reservation there. If something is actually wrong, it’ll usually show up inside the official account too.

And whatever you do, don’t impulse-Google a customer service number. Scammers sometimes create fake support lines designed to collect payment information from stressed-out travelers trying to fix a problem quickly. Basically, if a travel message suddenly makes your heart rate spike, that’s the moment to slow down.

A real airline might delay your flight. A scammer wants you to panic before you notice the email address ends in something bizarre like “support-air-help247.net.”

 Add Reader’s Digest on Google!

Sources:

  • Business Wire: “McAfee Research Finds 1 in 3 Travelers Have Faced a Travel Scam as Rising Costs Create New Opportunities for Fraud”
  • Federal Trade Commission: “How are scammers trying to reach you?”
  • AAA Newsroom: “45 Million Americans Planning Memorial Day Weekend Getaways”
  • Better Business Bureau: “Vacation Rental Booking Scams”

The post The Sneaky Summer Travel Scam Hitting Inboxes Right Before Memorial Day appeared first on Reader's Digest.

articles.title: A Brady Bunch Star Just Explained What Sudden Fame Felt Like Before Social Media
articles.content:
巴里·威廉姆斯14岁时，《布雷迪家庭》让他成为了每个人的“大哥”，这份“工作”伴随着喇叭裤和那个模拟时代的“病毒式传播”。

“我们从各自的生活中走出，做一件我们都热爱的事——它专业、专注、聚精会神——然后走向公众，”这位70年代的青少年偶像在5月20日播出的《Magnificent Others》节目中告诉碎南瓜乐队主唱比利·科根。“我们的生活因此发生了巨大的变化，因为将这一切与成长和青少年时期融合起来，是一次非常深刻的体验。”

巅峰时期，《布雷迪家庭》每周吸引多达4500万观众——这个数字足以让今天的流媒体收视榜为之侧目。Netflix上周全球排名第一的节目、凯文·哈特的吐槽大会，获得了1350万观看量。不同时代，不同衡量标准——但对比依然惊人。威廉姆斯和布雷迪家族的其他成员拥有大片级别的收视覆盖率，却没有一张自拍、一次赞助帖或公关推广。

“现在的所谓名人或网红达人……他们中的绝大多数都无法持久，”这位演员告诉科根。“你不会一遍又一遍地反复观看这些东西。”他说得有道理。《布雷迪家庭》自1969年以来基本上从未停止过重播。用互联网术语来说，这几乎是永生的。

而且这种 fame 也不会真正消退。威廉姆斯说，陌生人仍然会走进他常去的早餐店，坐到他的座位上，像老朋友一样和他聊天。说实话，这既甜蜜又有些超现实。这是一种永久性的、低调的 celebrity，早 在社交媒体学会按小时“制造”准社会关系之前就已存在。

威廉姆斯现年71岁，愉快地居住在密苏里州的一个湖边，他深知昙花一现的名声是怎么回事。毕竟，这正是当年扮演格雷格·布雷迪短暂化身“约翰尼·布拉沃”的人——一个口香糖流行歌手，创造这个角色的原因几乎仅仅是一位星探觉得他穿配套西装很好看。

转眼到现在，他成了接受真正摇滚明星采访的人。他给科根的最佳建议是什么？“上车吧。待在车上。热爱这趟旅程。这是我们唯一能拥有的旅程。”
articles.summary:
Barry Williams was 14 when The Brady Bunch turned him into everyone’s big brother, a job that came with bell-bottoms and the very analog version of going viral.

“We went from being who we were in our lives and then doing something that we all enjoyed doing, but was professional and focused and concentrated, and then went out into public,” the ’70s teen idol told Smashing Pumpkins frontman Billy Corgan on the May 20 episode of The Magnificent Others. “And our lives were changed dramatically by that, because that’s a very profound experience to assimilate with growing up and being a teenager.”

At its peak, The Brady Bunch was pulling up to 45 million viewers a week, which is the kind of number that makes today’s streaming charts blink twice. Netflix’s No. 1 global show last week, The Roast of Kevin Hart, drew 13.5 million views. Different era, different metric—but still. Williams and the rest of the Brady clan had blockbuster reach without a single selfie, sponsored post or PR rollout.

“The large majority of the people that are like famous now or influencer people now … they’re not going to be able to sustain,” the actor told Corgan. “You’re not going to be watching these things in reruns over and over and over again.” He’s got a point. The Brady Bunch has basically never stopped rerunning since 1969. In internet years, that’s practically immortal.

And that kind of fame doesn’t really wear off, either. Williams says strangers still slide into his booth at his favorite breakfast spot and start chatting like they’ve known him forever. Which, honestly, is both sweet and a little surreal. It’s the kind of permanent, low-grade celebrity that existed long before social media figured out how to manufacture parasocial relationships by the hour.

Williams, now 71 and happily settled on a lake in Missouri, knows a thing or two about fleeting stardom. After all, this is the same guy who once played Greg Brady’s short-lived alter ego “Johnny Bravo,” a bubblegum pop star created almost entirely because a talent scout thought he looked good in a matching suit.

Cut to today, he’s the one being interviewed by an actual rocker. The best advice he had for Corgan? “Get on a ride. Stay on the ride. Love the ride. It’s the only one we get.”
